content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 5

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Date:** 2026-03-05 | **Verses:** 19 | **Total Disagreements:** 28

---

## Summary Table

| # | Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | 1 | Rendering of qol alah | "voice of an oath" | "public adjuration to testify" | "public call to testify" | LOGOS — "public adjuration to testify" |
| 2 | 1 | "Does not speak up" vs "does not disclose" | "does not disclose it" | "does not speak up" | "fails to speak up" | LOGOS — "does not speak up" |
| 3 | 1 | Consequence of silence | "he shall bear his iniquity" | "he shall bear his iniquity" | "that person bears the consequences of their sin" | EMET/LOGOS — "he shall bear his iniquity" |
| 4 | 2 | "Swarming things" vs "swarming creatures" vs "crawling creature" | "unclean swarming things" | "unclean swarming creatures" | "unclean crawling creature" | LOGOS — "unclean swarming creatures" |
| 5 | 2 | Guilt incurrence phrasing | "he incurs guilt" | "he becomes guilty" | "they are guilty" | LOGOS — "he incurs guilt" |
| 6 | 3 | "Unclean" vs "defiled" | "unclean" | "unclean" | "defiled" | EMET/LOGOS — "unclean" |
| 7 | 4 | Rendering of le-vatte sefatayim | "uttering rashly with his lips" | "uttering rashly with his lips" | "thoughtlessly takes an oath to do something" | EMET/LOGOS — "uttering rashly with his lips" |
| 8 | 5 | Confession object | "confess that in which he has sinned" | "confess the sin he has committed" | "confess the specific sin they have committed" | LOGOS — "confess the sin he has committed" |
| 9 | 5–13 | chattat = "sin offering" vs "purification offering" | sin offering | sin offering | purification offering | EMET/LOGOS — "sin offering" (SOPHIA ruling: Lev 4, upheld) |
| 10 | 6 | Rendering of ashamah (vv. 6–7) | "his guilt payment [ashamah]" | "his guilt offering" | "their penalty" | EMET — "his guilt payment" (ashamah ≠ asham sacrifice) |
| 11 | 6 | "Female goat" vs "goat" | "female goat [se'irat izzim]" | "goat" | (not specified separately) | EMET — "female goat" (se'irat izzim specifies sex) |
| 12 | 7 | Affordability idiom | "his hand cannot reach enough for a lamb" | "cannot afford a lamb" | "cannot afford a sheep" | LOGOS — "cannot afford a lamb" |
| 13 | 7 | "Turtledoves" vs "doves" | "turtledoves" | "turtledoves" | "doves" | EMET/LOGOS — "turtledoves" (torim = turtledoves specifically) |
| 14 | 8 | Rendering of malaq | "pinch its head from its neck" | "wring its head from its neck" | "wring its neck without completely severing it" | EMET — "pinch its head from its neck" (malaq = nip/pinch; PNEUMA omits explicit "head" and anatomical phrase) |
| 15 | 9 | "Wall" vs "side" of the altar | "wall of the altar [qir ha-mizbeach]" | "side of the altar" | "side of the altar" | EMET — "wall of the altar" (qir = wall/vertical face) |
| 16 | 11 | Affordability idiom (second tier) | "his hand cannot reach enough for two turtledoves..." | "cannot afford two turtledoves..." | "cannot afford two doves..." | LOGOS — "cannot afford two turtledoves..." |
| 17 | 11 | "Frankincense" vs "incense" for levonah | "incense [levonah]" | "frankincense" | "incense" | LOGOS — "frankincense" (levonah is specifically frankincense, Boswellia resin) |
| 18 | 12 | Rendering of azkarah | "its representative portion [azkaratah]" | "its memorial portion" | "a token portion" | LOGOS — "its memorial portion" (z-k-r root = remembrance; "representative" and "token" lose this) |
| 19 | 14 | Waw-connector and lemor | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" | "The LORD spoke to Moses, saying," | "The LORD spoke to Moses:" | EMET — "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" (preserves waw-consecutive and lemor) |
| 20 | 15 | Rendering of ma'al | "commits unfaithfulness [ki-yim'ol ma'al]" | "commits an act of unfaithfulness" | "commits a breach of sacred trust" | LOGOS — "commits an act of unfaithfulness" |
| 21 | 15 | Rendering of be-erkekha (v. 15) | "assessed by you [be-erkekha]" | "valued by you" | "assessed at the proper value" | LOGOS — "valued by you" (preserves priestly 2nd-person address) |
| 22 | 15 | "Guilt payment" vs "guilt offering" vs "penalty" (v. 15) | "his guilt offering [ashamah]" | "his guilt offering" | "their penalty" | LOGOS — "his guilt offering" (in v. 15 asham is now the technical term for the sacrifice) |
| 23 | 16 | "Make restitution" vs "make full restitution" | "make restitution" | "make restitution" | "make full restitution" | EMET/LOGOS — "make restitution" (shillem does not add "full"; the one-fifth surcharge already signals completeness) |
| 24 | 17 | "Though he did not know it" phrasing | "and he did not know [ve-lo yada]" | "though he did not know it" | "even if they were not aware of it" | LOGOS — "though he did not know it" |
| 25 | 17 | "Shall bear his iniquity" (v. 17) | "shall bear his iniquity [ve-nasa avono]" | "shall bear his iniquity" | "bear the weight of their wrongdoing" | EMET/LOGOS — "shall bear his iniquity" |
| 26 | 18 | Rendering of shgagato | "his error [shgagato] that he committed unintentionally" | "the error that he committed unintentionally" | "what they did in ignorance without realizing it" | LOGOS — "the error that he committed unintentionally" |
| 27 | 18 | "Valued by you" vs "assessed by you" vs "valued at the proper amount" | "assessed by you" | "valued in silver" | "valued at the proper amount" | EMET — "valued by you" (preserves priestly 2nd-person address; "in silver" from v. 15 need not be repeated) |
| 28 | 19 | Emphatic asham asham rendering | "he has indeed incurred guilt [asham asham]" | "he has indeed incurred guilt" | "they were genuinely liable" | EMET/LOGOS — "he has indeed incurred guilt before the LORD" |

---

## Detailed Disagreement Records

---

### D-LEV005-01 | Verse 1 | Rendering of qol alah

**Hebrew:** קוֹל אָלָה (qol alah)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "the voice of an oath" (literal; preserves the idiom) |
| LOGOS | "a public adjuration to testify" (contextual; forensic gloss added) |
| PNEUMA | "a public call to testify" (paraphrase; loses the oath/alah vocabulary) |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS. The forensic setting of qol alah — a formal judicial summons with self-imprecation upon those who withhold testimony — is best captured by "adjuration" (stronger than "call"; carries the coercive oath-force of alah). EMET's "voice of an oath" is accurate but opaque to modern readers; "voice" for qol is not idiomatic in English legal contexts. PNEUMA's "call" loses the alah (oath/curse) dimension entirely. The word "testify" in LOGOS is an interpretive addition, but correctly contextualizes lo yaggid (failure to come forward as witness) that follows.

---

### D-LEV005-02 | Verse 1 | "Does not disclose" vs "does not speak up"

**Hebrew:** וְלֹא יַגִּיד (ve-lo yaggid, hiphil of nagad: "to make known / to disclose / to announce")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "does not disclose it" |
| LOGOS | "does not speak up" |
| PNEUMA | "fails to speak up" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("does not speak up"). "Speak up" is idiomatic English for public testimony and captures both the voluntary character of the act and its context (a legal/community setting). "Disclose" (EMET) is accurate but formal; "speak up" is more naturally collocated with the idea of testimony before an authority. PNEUMA's "fails to speak up" is acceptable but "fails" introduces a nuance of inability or neglect not quite as precise as the deliberate withholding implied by ve-lo yaggid.

---

### D-LEV005-03 | Verse 1 | Consequence — "bear his iniquity" vs "bears the consequences"

**Hebrew:** וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ (ve-nasa avono)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "he shall bear his iniquity" |
| LOGOS | "he shall bear his iniquity" |
| PNEUMA | "that person bears the consequences of their sin" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS. The phrase nasa avon is a fixed legal-theological expression throughout the Pentateuch. "Bear his iniquity" preserves the vocabulary of avon (iniquity, moral-legal guilt) that links to the broader guilt system of Leviticus. PNEUMA's "bears the consequences of their sin" is interpretive (consequences ≠ bearing; sin ≠ avon) and loses the precise legal register. Consistency across the Pentateuch requires the established formula.

---

### D-LEV005-04 | Verse 2 | "Swarming things" vs "swarming creatures" vs "crawling creature"

**Hebrew:** שֶׁרֶץ טָמֵא (sheretz tame)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "unclean swarming things" |
| LOGOS | "unclean swarming creatures" |
| PNEUMA | "unclean crawling creature" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("unclean swarming creatures"). The Hebrew sheretz covers a wide range of small teeming creatures (insects, reptiles, small crawling animals); "swarming" captures the root's connotation of numerous, rapid movement better than "crawling" (PNEUMA), which is too narrow. "Creatures" (LOGOS) is preferable to "things" (EMET) since these are living animals. Note: PNEUMA uses the singular, losing the collective breadth of the category.

---

### D-LEV005-05 | Verse 2 | Guilt incurrence phrasing

**Hebrew:** וְאָשֵׁם (ve-ashem, "and he incurs guilt / becomes guilty")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "he incurs guilt" |
| LOGOS | "he becomes guilty" |
| PNEUMA | "they are guilty" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET ("he incurs guilt"). The verb ashem (Qal perfect with waw-consecutive) describes the active incurring of guilt, not merely a resultant state. "Incurs guilt" captures this dynamic better than "becomes guilty" (LOGOS, more stative) or "are guilty" (PNEUMA, entirely stative and in plural). Consistency with the chapter's asham root vocabulary also supports "incurs guilt."

---

### D-LEV005-06 | Verse 3 | "Unclean" vs "defiled" for tame

**Hebrew:** יִטְמָא (yitma, "becomes unclean")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "becomes unclean" |
| LOGOS | "becomes unclean" |
| PNEUMA | "becomes defiled" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS ("unclean"). The Hebrew root t-m-' (tame/tumah) is the standard ritual uncleanness vocabulary of Leviticus. "Unclean" has been the consistent rendering since Leviticus 11–15 (established vocabulary). "Defiled" (PNEUMA) has strong moral connotations that are not always present in tame; ritual uncleanness in vv. 2–3 may be entirely accidental and non-moral. Maintaining "unclean" preserves the technical register.

---

### D-LEV005-07 | Verse 4 | Rendering of le-vatte sefatayim / rash oath

**Hebrew:** לְבַטֵּא בִשְׂפָתַיִם (le-vatte bi-sefatayim, "to utter rashly with the lips")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "uttering rashly with his lips" |
| LOGOS | "uttering rashly with his lips" |
| PNEUMA | "thoughtlessly takes an oath to do something" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS ("uttering rashly with his lips"). The Hebrew contains the specific anatomical reference sefatayim ("lips") and the root b-t-' ("to speak rashly"). These deserve preservation: the lips are the organ of speech and oath, and their explicit mention signals the gravity of spoken commitment. PNEUMA's "thoughtlessly takes an oath" loses both the lips and the specific verbal action (le-vatte = to blurt/utter rashly, not merely to take an oath thoughtlessly).

---

### D-LEV005-08 | Verse 5 | Object of confession

**Hebrew:** אֲשֶׁר חָטָא עָלֶיהָ (asher chata aleiha, "that in which he sinned / the sin he committed")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "that in which he has sinned" |
| LOGOS | "the sin he has committed" |
| PNEUMA | "the specific sin they have committed" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("the sin he has committed"). LOGOS is clear and idiomatic; "the sin he has committed" accurately renders the relative clause without over-literalizing (EMET: "that in which he has sinned" is awkward). PNEUMA's addition of "specific" is an interpretive expansion (albeit a valid one, as EMET's notes on ve-hitvaddah confirm the specificity requirement) — but it adds a word not in the Hebrew. The specificity is implicit in the act of naming the sin, not made explicit by an adverb.

---

### D-LEV005-09 | Verses 6, 7, 9, 11, 12, 13 | chattat = "purification offering" (PNEUMA) vs "sin offering" (EMET, LOGOS)

**Hebrew:** חַטָּאת (chattat)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | sin offering |
| LOGOS | sin offering |
| PNEUMA | purification offering (throughout vv. 6–13) |

**SOPHIA:** Upholds prior ruling (Lev 4). "Sin offering" is the SOPHIA Bible's established rendering for chattat. PNEUMA's "purification offering" (following Milgrom's influential scholarship) is noted as a legitimate alternative in academic translation — chattat does have a purgation/purification function — but the SOPHIA Bible prioritizes consistency across the Levitical corpus over alignment with any single scholarly school. This ruling stands for all subsequent occurrences of chattat in Leviticus.

---

### D-LEV005-10 | Verses 6–7 | Rendering of ashamah (guilt-liability noun) vs asham (sacrifice)

**Hebrew:** אֲשָׁמוֹ / אֲשָׁמָם (ashamah/ashamam — abstract guilt-liability noun in vv. 6–7)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "his guilt payment [ashamah]" / "his guilt payment [ashamam]" |
| LOGOS | "his guilt offering" |
| PNEUMA | "their penalty" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET ("his guilt payment"). The term in vv. 6–7 is the ashamah/ashamam — the abstract noun form expressing the guilt-liability or reparation owed, not yet the specific asham sacrifice. The actual sacrifice offered is named chattat (sin offering). To call it "his guilt offering" (LOGOS) conflates the guilt-liability concept with the technical guilt offering that is not introduced until v. 15; this creates a category confusion that EMET correctly avoids. PNEUMA's "penalty" is too vague and loses the asham root that unifies the chapter. "Guilt payment" captures both the liability (guilt) and the transactional element (payment/reparation) of ashamah.

---

### D-LEV005-11 | Verse 6 | "Female goat" vs "goat"

**Hebrew:** שְׂעִירַת עִזִּים (se'irat izzim, "female kid/goat of the goats")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "female goat" |
| LOGOS | "goat" |
| PNEUMA | (combined as "a female animal from the flock — either a lamb or a goat") |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET ("female goat"). The Hebrew se'irat (feminine form of sa'ir, "goat/he-goat") specifically designates a female goat, as does kivshah (female lamb) for the alternative. The sex specification is meaningful in the sacrificial system (contrast: male ram required for the asham, vv. 15, 18; female animals for the sin offering). LOGOS's "goat" loses this distinction.

---

### D-LEV005-12 | Verse 7 | Affordability idiom

**Hebrew:** וְאִם לֹא תַגִּיעַ יָדוֹ (ve-im lo taggiya yado, "if his hand does not reach")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "if his hand cannot reach enough for a lamb" |
| LOGOS | "if he cannot afford a lamb" |
| PNEUMA | "if they cannot afford a sheep" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("if he cannot afford a lamb"). "Cannot afford" is natural English for the Hebrew idiom of the "hand not reaching" (financial incapacity). EMET's literal "hand cannot reach enough" is awkward. PNEUMA's "sheep" for se (lamb) is imprecise; the Hebrew se specifically refers to a young sheep (lamb), not a generic sheep of any age.

---

### D-LEV005-13 | Verse 7 | "Turtledoves" vs "doves"

**Hebrew:** תֹּרִים (torim, "turtledoves," singular tor)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "turtledoves" |
| LOGOS | "turtledoves" |
| PNEUMA | "doves" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS ("turtledoves"). The Hebrew tor is specifically the turtledove (Streptopelia turtur), a wild migratory bird distinct from the domesticated pigeon/dove (yonah). The distinction between the two species is meaningful (wild vs. domestic, each with different availability by season). "Doves" (PNEUMA) conflates the two categories and loses the lexical specificity.

---

### D-LEV005-14 | Verse 8 | Rendering of malaq — "pinch" vs "wring"

**Hebrew:** וּמָלַק אֶת-רֹאשׁוֹ מִמּוּל עָרְפּוֹ וְלֹא יַבְדִּיל (u-malaq et-rosho mi-mul orpo ve-lo yavdil)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "he shall pinch its head from its neck but shall not sever it" |
| LOGOS | "He shall wring its head from its neck but shall not sever it" |
| PNEUMA | "He shall wring its neck without completely severing it" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET ("pinch its head from its neck but shall not sever it"). Three differences are present: (a) malaq = pinch/nip (EMET) vs. wring (LOGOS/PNEUMA); (b) the explicit object "its head" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "its neck" (PNEUMA, losing the head/neck distinction); (c) "from its neck" rendering mi-mul orpo. On (a): malaq is a nipping/pinching action, not a wringing (which implies twisting). "Pinch" is etymologically more precise; "wring" is the standard English translation tradition but may overstate the rotational force involved. On (b): PNEUMA's "its neck" is a significant loss — the Hebrew explicitly specifies the head (rosho) as the object being separated from the nape (orpo), which is anatomically crucial to the blood-manipulation technique described in v. 9. EMET preserves both the action and the anatomy.

---

### D-LEV005-15 | Verse 9 | "Wall" vs "side" of the altar

**Hebrew:** קִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (qir ha-mizbeach)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "wall of the altar" |
| LOGOS | "side of the altar" |
| PNEUMA | "side of the altar" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET ("wall of the altar"). The Hebrew qir means "wall" — specifically the vertical face or surface of the altar. "Wall" is the more precise rendering: the blood is sprinkled against the vertical face, not merely at the "side" (which could suggest a lateral position rather than the wall surface). LOGOS and PNEUMA's "side" follows common English translations (ESV, NIV) but is less precise. The wall-surface specificity is important for understanding how blood manipulation differs between the bird sin offering and the quadruped sin offering.

---

### D-LEV005-16 | Verse 11 | Second affordability idiom

**Hebrew:** וְאִם לֹא-תַשִּׂיג יָדוֹ לִשְׁתֵּי תֹרִים (ve-im lo-tassig yado li-shtei torim)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "if his hand cannot reach enough for two turtledoves or two young pigeons" |
| LOGOS | "if he cannot afford two turtledoves or two young pigeons" |
| PNEUMA | "if they cannot afford two doves or two young pigeons" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS for the same reasons as D-LEV005-12 above ("cannot afford") and D-LEV005-13 ("turtledoves" not "doves").

---

### D-LEV005-17 | Verse 11 | "Frankincense" vs "incense" for levonah

**Hebrew:** לְבֹנָה (levonah)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "incense [levonah]" |
| LOGOS | "frankincense" |
| PNEUMA | "incense" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("frankincense"). The Hebrew levonah is the specific aromatic resin from the Boswellia tree — frankincense — not the general category "incense" (which would cover any aromatic substance burned). "Frankincense" is more precise and is the standard rendering (ESV, NASB, KJV) for levonah throughout the Levitical legislation. The distinction matters: the prohibition is against this specific costly resin, which is an adornment of wealth. "Incense" is ambiguous.

---

### D-LEV005-18 | Verse 12 | Rendering of azkarah — "memorial" vs "representative" vs "token"

**Hebrew:** אַזְכָּרָתָהּ (azkaratah, "its memorial/remembrance portion")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "its representative portion [azkaratah]" |
| LOGOS | "its memorial portion" |
| PNEUMA | "a token portion" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("its memorial portion"). The root z-k-r ("to remember") is the basis of azkarah; "memorial" preserves this etymology and its covenantal resonance (the burning handful brings the offerer to remembrance before the LORD). "Representative" (EMET) captures the functional relationship to the whole offering but loses the remembrance dimension. "Token" (PNEUMA) deflates the theological weight of the term, reducing a covenantal act of divine remembrance to a mere symbolic gesture.

---

### D-LEV005-19 | Verse 14 | Narrative connector and lemor

**Hebrew:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" |
| LOGOS | "The LORD spoke to Moses, saying," |
| PNEUMA | "The LORD spoke to Moses:" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET ("And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:"). (a) The waw-consecutive (And) marks the structural shift from the chattat legislation to the asham legislation — it should be preserved. (b) PNEUMA omits "saying" (lemor), which in biblical narrative introduces direct speech; its omission creates a formal gap. LOGOS preserves "saying" but omits the waw connector. EMET preserves both elements.

---

### D-LEV005-20 | Verse 15 | Rendering of ma'al

**Hebrew:** כִּי יִמְעֹל מַעַל (ki yim'ol ma'al, "if/when he commits a trespass")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "commits unfaithfulness [ki-yim'ol ma'al]" |
| LOGOS | "commits an act of unfaithfulness" |
| PNEUMA | "commits a breach of sacred trust" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("commits an act of unfaithfulness"). The construction ki yim'ol ma'al is an infinitive absolute (verb + cognate noun), a Hebrew emphatic idiom: "he trespasses a trespass." LOGOS's "commits an act of unfaithfulness" captures the substantive weight of the doubled construction. "Unfaithfulness" is the established SOPHIA rendering for ma'al (see established terms). PNEUMA's "breach of sacred trust" is an admissible paraphrase but imports "trust" language not present in the Hebrew root and loses the ma'al vocabulary.

---

### D-LEV005-21 | Verse 15 | Rendering of be-erkekha (priestly valuation)

**Hebrew:** בְּעֶרְכְּךָ (be-erkekha, "according to your valuation / valued by you")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "assessed by you [be-erkekha]" |
| LOGOS | "valued by you" |
| PNEUMA | "assessed at the proper value" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("valued by you"). Both EMET and LOGOS preserve the second-person address to the priestly assessor, which is the correct grammatical rendering. PNEUMA's "at the proper value" depersonalizes the transaction and loses the priestly role. Between EMET ("assessed") and LOGOS ("valued"), "valued" aligns better with the erekh root (erakh = value/valuation), while "assessed" is more procedural. "Valued by you in silver shekels according to the shekel of the sanctuary" (LOGOS) is the fullest and most precise rendering.

---

### D-LEV005-22 | Verse 15 | "Guilt payment" vs "guilt offering" vs "penalty" in Section B (asham as sacrifice)

**Hebrew:** לַאֲשָׁם (la-asham, "as a guilt offering")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "his guilt offering [ashamah]" (note: EMET labels it ashamah in the bracket but translates correctly) |
| LOGOS | "his guilt offering" |
| PNEUMA | "their penalty" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("his guilt offering"). In v. 15 the term is clearly the named sacrifice asham (guilt offering), distinct from the ashamah guilt-liability noun of vv. 6–7. Both EMET and LOGOS render it "guilt offering" in their translations (EMET's bracket note is an explanatory aside). PNEUMA's "penalty" is again too vague for the specific sacrificial term. Note this is a different application of the asham root than D-LEV005-10 (vv. 6–7) where "guilt payment" was ruled; the distinction between ashamah (abstract liability noun) in vv. 6–7 and asham (sacrifice name) in vv. 14–19 is now established.

---

### D-LEV005-23 | Verse 16 | "Make restitution" vs "make full restitution"

**Hebrew:** וְשִׁלֵּם (ve-shillem, piel of shalom: "and he shall make restitution / repay")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "make restitution" |
| LOGOS | "make restitution" |
| PNEUMA | "make full restitution" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS ("make restitution"). The Hebrew shillem (piel of shalom) means to make good, to repay, to compensate — it does not inherently carry "full" as an explicit modifier. The one-fifth surcharge that follows already signals that the restitution is complete plus a premium. Adding "full" (PNEUMA) is redundant with the surcharge and imports an adjective not present in the Hebrew.

---

### D-LEV005-24 | Verse 17 | "Though he did not know it" phrasing

**Hebrew:** וְלֹא יָדַע (ve-lo yada, "and he did not know")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "and he did not know [ve-lo yada]" |
| LOGOS | "though he did not know it" |
| PNEUMA | "even if they were not aware of it" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("though he did not know it"). LOGOS's "though he did not know it" captures both the concessive force (the ignorance does not eliminate the guilt) and the straightforward Hebrew of ve-lo yada. EMET's "and he did not know" is accurate but lacks the concessive connective. PNEUMA's "even if they were not aware" is a valid paraphrase but "were not aware" is weaker than "did not know" for lo yada, and the plural is not warranted.

---

### D-LEV005-25 | Verse 17 | "Shall bear his iniquity" (second occurrence)

**Hebrew:** וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ (ve-nasa avono)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "shall bear his iniquity" |
| LOGOS | "shall bear his iniquity" |
| PNEUMA | "bear the weight of their wrongdoing" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS ("shall bear his iniquity"). See D-LEV005-03. Same formula, same ruling. Consistency requires the established "bear his iniquity" for nasa avon throughout.

---

### D-LEV005-26 | Verse 18 | Rendering of shgagato ("his error / inadvertent act")

**Hebrew:** שְׁגָגָתוֹ (shgagato, "his inadvertence / his error / his unintentional transgression")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "the error that he committed unintentionally [shgagato]" (brackets note the term) |
| LOGOS | "the error that he committed unintentionally" |
| PNEUMA | "what they did in ignorance without realizing it" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS ("the error that he committed unintentionally"). LOGOS and EMET converge on the same rendering; "error ... unintentionally" captures the shgagah concept (inadvertent transgression) consistently with the established bi-shgagah rendering elsewhere (Lev 4). PNEUMA's "what they did in ignorance without realizing it" is verbose and substitutes "ignorance" for the sharper category of error/inadvertence; shgagah is not precisely the same as ignorance (agnoia) — it is an inadvertent blunder, not merely a state of not-knowing.

---

### D-LEV005-27 | Verse 18 | "Valued by you" vs "valued in silver" vs "valued at the proper amount"

**Hebrew:** בְּעֶרְכְּךָ (be-erkekha, again; same as v. 15 but here without explicit "silver shekels" or "sanctuary shekel")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "assessed by you" |
| LOGOS | "valued in silver" |
| PNEUMA | "valued at the proper amount" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET with modification ("valued by you"). In v. 18 the Hebrew repeats be-erkekha without the full monetary specification of v. 15 (no "silver shekels" or "shekel of the sanctuary" attached). LOGOS renders "valued in silver" — importing the silver from v. 15 — which is an interpretive supplement. PNEUMA's "at the proper amount" depersonalizes the priestly role. EMET's "assessed by you" preserves the priestly address. However, consistent with the ruling in D-LEV005-21, "assessed" is adjusted to "valued" for erekh consistency. Final ruling: "valued by you" for v. 18.

---

### D-LEV005-28 | Verse 19 | Emphatic asham asham construction

**Hebrew:** אָשֹׁם אָשַׁם לַיהוָה (ashom asham la-YHWH, infinitive absolute: "he has certainly/indeed incurred guilt before the LORD")

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "he has indeed incurred guilt [asham asham] before the LORD" |
| LOGOS | "he has indeed incurred guilt before the LORD" |
| PNEUMA | "they were genuinely liable before the LORD" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS ("he has indeed incurred guilt before the LORD"). The infinitive absolute construction (ashom asham, doubling the root for emphasis) is a prominent rhetorical device that should be reflected in the English by a strong emphatic adverb ("indeed"). "He has indeed incurred guilt before the LORD" accomplishes this. PNEUMA's "were genuinely liable" substitutes a different vocabulary ("liable" for "incurred guilt") and loses the legal-theological asham vocabulary that has governed the entire second half of the chapter. Additionally, the past tense "were" for PNEUMA shifts the temporal frame awkwardly.

---

## Cross-Reference Notes

**Chattat/asham terminology overlap (vv. 6–7):** The most structurally significant terminological issue in the chapter. In vv. 6–7, the offerer brings an ashamah (guilt-liability reparation) to the LORD, and the actual sacrifice is a chattat (sin offering). This has led to inconsistency across translators: LOGOS calls the ashamah of v. 6 a "guilt offering" (conflating it with the technical asham of vv. 14–19), while PNEUMA calls the chattat a "purification offering." SOPHIA's rulings on D-LEV005-09 and D-LEV005-10 together resolve this: in vv. 6–7 use "guilt payment" (ashamah) + "sin offering" (chattat); in vv. 14–19 use "guilt offering" (asham).

**PNEUMA's gender-inclusive pronouns:** PNEUMA consistently uses they/their/them for the individual offender throughout Leviticus 5. While this is a legitimate interpretive choice for modern readers (acknowledging that nefesh applies to all persons), the SOPHIA Bible uses masculine singular pronouns with "a person" as subject, following Hebrew grammatical convention. This affects nearly every verse and is logged as a standing systemic difference rather than verse-by-verse entries.

**PNEUMA's "purification offering":** PNEUMA uses "purification offering" for chattat in every occurrence in this chapter (vv. 6, 7, 9, 11, 12, 13), contra the SOPHIA ruling established in Leviticus 4. This is a repeat violation of the established consensus ruling and is logged collectively under D-LEV005-09.
